helping_a_heroorg.how_did_you_hear_about_helping_a_hero:
I first heard about Helping A Hero through a fellow veteran in my adaptive sports group, Sgt. Michael Ramirez (USMC, retired). We were at a Team RWB cycling event in San Antonio in early 2023, and during a break he mentioned that he had attended one of your ceremonies where a double amputee received a specially adapted home. He encouraged me to look you up because my wife and I have been struggling with accessibility in our current house.

After that conversation, I searched online for more information. I found Helping A Hero’s website and watched several of the home dedication videos you have posted, including the one from Houston where you partnered with a local builder to provide a fully wheelchair-accessible home. I also started following your Facebook page, where I saw posts about the home application process, upcoming events, and stories of other wounded veterans who had received home grants.

A few months later, I attended a military appreciation luncheon hosted by a local church (Oak Hills Community Church in San Antonio). One of the guest speakers was a representative from Helping A Hero who shared more details about the program, the selection process, and the types of modifications you commonly include in the homes (wider doorways, roll‑in showers, lower countertops, etc.). That presentation, combined with what I had already seen online and heard from Michael, convinced me that your organization might be a good fit for my situation, so I decided to complete this application.
helping_a_heroorg.do_you_know_any_veteran_who_has_received:
I personally know one veteran who has received support from Helping A Hero:

1. **SSG Michael "Mike" Ramirez, USMC (Ret.)** – Mike and I met through the VA adaptive sports program and later reconnected through Team RWB in San Antonio. Mike is a Marine Corps veteran who lost his right leg above the knee due to an IED blast in Afghanistan in 2011. He told me that he was awarded a Helping A Hero home grant several years ago (I believe around 2016 or 2017) for a home in the Houston, TX area.

He described how Helping A Hero worked with a local builder to design a house that would allow him to move around safely and independently in his wheelchair, including:
- A zero‑step entry from the garage into the house
- Extra‑wide interior doorways and hallways
- An open floor plan so he could easily maneuver his wheelchair
- A fully accessible bathroom with a roll‑in shower, grab bars, and a handheld showerhead
- Lowered counters and accessible storage in the kitchen so he could cook for himself without constantly needing assistance

Mike has said many times that receiving that home dramatically improved his quality of life and reduced his daily pain and fatigue because he no longer has to struggle with stairs and tight spaces. He is actually the one who strongly encouraged me to apply, based on his personal experience with Helping A Hero and how responsive and respectful he felt the organization was throughout his application and home‑building process.

Aside from Mike, I don’t personally know any other veterans who have received a Helping A Hero home, but I have watched several of the recipient stories and dedication ceremonies on your website and Facebook page, which helped me better understand the impact your homes and grants can have on veterans and their families.

support_from_other_organizations.have_you_received_any_post_injury_suppor:
Yes, I have received support from several nonprofit and veteran‑related organizations since my injury. I’ll list them by organization and describe the type of assistance they provided:

1. **Wounded Warrior Project (WWP)**  
   - **Type of support:** Mental health counseling, peer support, and family retreat.  
   - **Details:** After my medical retirement from the Army in 2018 due to a spinal cord injury and lower‑extremity nerve damage, I enrolled with Wounded Warrior Project. They connected me with a combat stress recovery program that included individual counseling sessions and group workshops focused on PTSD, chronic pain management, and reintegration into civilian life. My wife was also able to attend a weekend family retreat at a ranch outside of Austin, TX, which helped both of us learn better communication and coping strategies. WWP has not provided any housing‑related support, but their mental health resources and peer support groups have been a huge help in managing depression and anxiety related to my injury and limitations.

2. **Fisher House Foundation**  
   - **Type of support:** Temporary lodging for my wife and mother during my initial hospitalization and surgeries.  
   - **Details:** When I was first injured and undergoing multiple surgeries and rehab at Brooke Army Medical Center (BAMC) in San Antonio, my wife and my mother stayed at a Fisher House on the hospital campus. This allowed them to be close by during the most critical stages of my recovery without the financial burden of paying for a hotel for several months. The Fisher House provided a safe, supportive environment with other military families going through similar experiences. While this was not permanent housing support, it made a tremendous difference during that period and allowed my family to focus on my recovery instead of worrying about lodging costs.

3. **Gary Sinise Foundation – R.I.S.E. program (application stage only)**  
   - **Type of support:** Information and guidance; no financial support or home grant received.  
   - **Details:** We explored the possibility of applying to the Gary Sinise Foundation’s R.I.S.E. program for a specially adapted smart home. We attended an informational session and had one preliminary phone consultation with one of their case managers in 2020. Ultimately, we did not move forward with a full application at that time because my medical situation and income were still in flux, and we were not yet certain where we wanted to settle long‑term. We have not received any funding or home modifications from them, but they did provide helpful guidance about what features are typically included in an adapted home and what documentation is usually required.

4. **Homes for Our Troops (HFOT) – Home modification grant (limited)**  
   - **Type of support:** Small grant for accessibility modifications to our current home.  
   - **Details:** In 2021, we received a modest grant from Homes for Our Troops to help with some basic accessibility changes to our current rental home in San Antonio. The landlord agreed to allow certain modifications as long as they could be reversed or did not structurally alter the property. With HFOT’s help, we were able to:
     - Install one small ramp at the back patio step.
     - Add a few grab bars in the bathroom near the toilet and in the tub/shower area.
     - Replace round doorknobs with lever‑style handles on a few interior doors.
   These changes improved safety somewhat, but the overall layout of the house is still not wheelchair‑friendly (narrow hallway, small bathroom, carpet in most rooms). HFOT’s support was helpful but limited due to the constraints of renting rather than owning.

5. **Team Red, White & Blue (Team RWB)**  
   - **Type of support:** Community, physical fitness activities, and social support.  
   - **Details:** Team RWB has not provided direct financial assistance, but they have played a big role in my emotional and physical recovery. Through their local San Antonio chapter, I participate in adaptive cycling events, low‑impact group workouts, and social gatherings. This has helped me rebuild a sense of camaraderie and purpose that I missed after leaving the military. It also encourages me to stay as active as my condition allows, which helps manage chronic pain and improves my overall mental health.

6. **Disabled American Veterans (DAV)**  
   - **Type of support:** Benefits counseling and transportation assistance.  
   - **Details:** DAV helped me navigate the VA benefits system when I was going through my initial disability rating process. They assisted with paperwork, explained what documentation I needed from my doctors, and helped me file an appeal when my first rating decision did not fully reflect the extent of my limitations. They have also provided occasional transportation to VA medical appointments when my wife’s work schedule made it difficult for her to drive me. While this support is not housing‑related, it has been very important in securing the benefits that help us cover our basic living expenses.

To summarize, I have received **emotional, logistical, and limited home‑modification support** from various nonprofit organizations, but I have **not** received a fully adapted home or a major home grant from any organization. Most of the assistance has been focused on mental health, temporary lodging, small accessibility improvements in our rental, and help navigating the VA system. Our current living situation remains only partially accessible, which is why I am seeking more comprehensive housing assistance through Helping A Hero.
